59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94

101	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian, lem:descent_through_birational_sigma, lem:albanese_eq_iff_symmetricPower_eq, thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, thm:rigidity_lemma}
102

105	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
106	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
107	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
108	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
109	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
110	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
111	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
112	
113	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
114	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
115	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
116	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
117	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
118	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
119	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
120	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
121	\end{theorem}

126	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
127	then gives the proof.

297	  \]
298	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
299	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
300	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
301	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
302	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
303	  \[
304	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
305	  \]
306	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
307	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
308	
309	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
310	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
311	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
312	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
313	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
314	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
315	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
316	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
317	\end{proof}

319	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
320	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
321	
322	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
323	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
324	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
325	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
326	
327	\begin{definition}\leanok
328	[Symmetric power of a curve]
329	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
330	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
331	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
332	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
333	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
334	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
335	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
336	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
337	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
338	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
339	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
340	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
341	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
342	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
343	  %  having the following properties:
344	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
345	  %        by \(S_r\);
346	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
347	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
348	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
349	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
350	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
351	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
352	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
353	  %  through \(\pi\).
354	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
355	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
356	
357	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
358	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
359	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
360	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
361	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
362	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
363	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
364	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
365	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
366	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
367	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
368	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
369	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
370	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).

389	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
390	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
391	
392	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
393	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
394	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
395	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
396	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
397	
398	\begin{lemma}\leanok
399	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
400	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
401	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
402	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
403	
404	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
405	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
406	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
407	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
408	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
409	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
410	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
411	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
412	
413	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
414	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
415	  \[
416	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
417	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
418	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
419	  \]
420	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
421	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
422	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
423	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
424	  \[
425	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
426	  \]
427	  such that
428	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
429	\end{lemma}
430	
431	\begin{proof}
432	  
433	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
434	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
435	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
436	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
437	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
438	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
439	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
440	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
441	  \[
442	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
443	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
444	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
445	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
446	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
447	  \]
448	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
449	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
450	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
451	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
452	\end{proof}

454	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
455	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}

458	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
459	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
460	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
461	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).

464	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]

474	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,

489	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
490	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
491	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
492	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
493	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold

513	
514	% NOTE (iter-199): the birationality proof below derives the generic-fibre
515	% identification from Riemann--Roch at \(\deg D = g\) (specifically
516	% \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = 1\) generically). Riemann--Roch is the Route C

527	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
528	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
529	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
530	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
531	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
532	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
533	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
534	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
535	
536	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
537	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
538	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
539	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
540	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
541	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
542	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
543	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
544	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
545	
546	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
547	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
548	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =

550	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
551	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
552	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
553	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).

555	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the

667	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
668	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
669	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
670	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
671	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
672	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
673	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
674	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
675	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
676	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
677	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
678	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
679	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
680	  
681	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
682	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
683	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
684	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
685	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
686	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
687	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
688	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
689	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
690	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
691	  \begin{enumerate}
692	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
693	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
694	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
695	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
696	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
697	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
698	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
699	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
700	      \[
701	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
702	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
703	      \]
704	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
705	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
706	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
707	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
708	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
709	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
710	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
711	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
712	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
713	      to a regular morphism
714	      \[
715	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
716	      \]
717	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
718	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
719	      \[
720	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
721	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
722	      \]
723	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
724	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
725	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
726	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
727	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
728	      \(\bar k\)-points,
729	      \[
730	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
731	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
732	      \]
733	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
734	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
735	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
736	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
737	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
738	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
739	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
740	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
741	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
742	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
743	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
744	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
745	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
746	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
747	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
748	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
749	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
750	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
751	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
752	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
753	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
754	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
755	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
756	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
757	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
758	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
759	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
760	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
761	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
762	  \end{enumerate}
763	  This completes the proof.
764	\end{proof}
765	
766	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
767	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
768	
769	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
770	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
771	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
772	fields are supplied by A.3:
773	\begin{itemize}
774	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
775	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
776	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
777	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
778	    refinement).
779	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
780	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
781	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
782	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
783	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
784	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
785	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
786	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
787	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
788	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
789	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
790	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
791	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
792	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
793	\end{itemize}
794	
795	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
796	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
797	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
798	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
799

872	\section{Out of scope}
873	\label{sec:albaneseup_out_of_scope}
874	
875	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Albanese universal property
876	(\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} = Milne Proposition~III~6.1) and the
877	four internal sub-lemmas of the symmetric-power proof
878	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism},
879	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
880	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map},
881	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
882	\cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}), plus the wiring of these into
883	a positive-genus Albanese witness. The following adjacent content is
884	explicitly out of scope:
885	\begin{itemize}
886	  \item \textbf{Representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (A.2.c).} Proved in
887	    \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} as
888	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}; this chapter only consumes it.
889	  \item \textbf{The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and the degree map
890	    (A.3).} The identity component refinement of the Picard scheme together
891	    with its degree map is the subject of a forthcoming chapter; this chapter
892	    consumes \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
893	    \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\) but does not construct it.
894	  \item \textbf{Milne's Theorem~3.2 (A.4.c).} Proved in
895	    \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
896	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only consumes it
897	    inside \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} to promote the
898	    rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism.
899	  \item \textbf{The scheme-theoretic construction of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\).}
900	    Mathlib has no \(g\)-th symmetric power of a scheme; the
901	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} target of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve} is a
902	    project-side sub-build (affine \(S_g\)-invariants
903	    \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\) on each open affine \(\Spec A \subseteq C\),
904	    glued by the standard open-cover patching of Milne~III.3
905	    Proposition~3.1 / Mumford~1970 II.7 \& III.11). The construction itself
906	    is a non-trivial dependency of A.4.d; this chapter \emph{specifies} the
907	    target but does not develop its construction.
908	  \item \textbf{The autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\) (Milne~III \S 6.6) and the
909	    moduli / Poincar\'e-bundle Albanese route.} The iter-174 draft of this
910	    chapter proposed a route via the Poincar\'e bundle of \(A\) and the
911	    autoduality of \(J\), which gates on the theorem of the cube. The cube
912	    was excised from the project in iter-163; the autoduality / moduli
913	    route is therefore demoted and replaced by the symmetric-power route
914	    of this chapter. A reference recording of the moduli wording is given
915	    in the ``Alternative route history'' \texttt{\%~NOTE} block at the end
916	    of this file.
917	  \item \textbf{Milne Proposition~III~6.4 / Remark~6.5 / Theorem~6.6} (the
918	    divisorial-correspondence universal property for \(C \times C \to A\),
919	    the identification of \((J, F)\) with the Albanese variety in the sense
920	    of Lang, and the autoduality \(J \cong J^\vee\)). Not needed by the
921	    symmetric-power route.
922	  \item \textbf{Weil's reconstruction of \(J\) from the rational law of
923	    composition on \(C^{(g)}\) (Milne~III \S 7).} The project does not need
924	    this alternative construction.
925	  \item \textbf{The genus-\(0\) case.} The standing hypothesis of this chapter
926	    is \(g(C) > 0\) (Milne's standing hypothesis of \S III.6). The genus-\(0\)
927	    case of the Albanese witness is supplied by

930	    shortcut; that construction is independent of and disjoint from the
931	    Pic\(^0\)-route of this chapter.
